elements.sent-2411-001: The dual of a planar graph $G$ is a planar graph $G^*$ that has a vertex for each face of $G$ and an edge for each pair of adjacent faces of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-002: The profound relationship between a planar graph and its dual has been the algorithmic basis for solving numerous (centralized) classical problems on planar graphs involving distances, flows, and cuts.
elements.sent-2411-003: In the distributed setting however, the only use of planar duality is for finding a recursive decomposition of $G$ [DISC 2017, STOC 2019].
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper, we initiate the study of distributed algorithms on dual planar graphs.
elements.sent-2411-005: Namely, we extend the distributed algorithmic toolkit (such as recursive decomposition and minor-aggregation) to work on the dual graph $G^*$.
elements.sent-2411-006: These tools can then facilitate various algorithms on $G$ by solving a suitable dual problem on $G^*$.
elements.sent-2411-007: Given a directed planar graph $G$ with positive and negative edge-lengths and hop-diameter $D$, our key result is an $\tilde O(D^2)$-round algorithm\footnote{The $\tilde{O (\cdot)$ notation is used to omit $\Poly \log n$ factors.}}
elements.sent-2411-008: Prior to our work, no $\tilde{O (\text{poly (D))$-round algorithm was known for Maximum $st$-Flow.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: We further obtain a $D\cdot n^{o(1)}$-rounds $(1-\epsilon)$-approximation algorithm for Maximum $st$-Flow on $G$ when $G$ is undirected and $s$ and $t$ lie on the same face.
elements.sent-2411-010: Finally, we give a near optimal $\tilde O(D)$-round algorithm for computing the weighted girth of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-011: We believe that the toolkit developed in this paper for exploiting planar duality will be used in future distributed algorithms for various other classical problems on planar graphs (as happened in the centralized setting).
elements.sent-2411-012: The main challenges in our work are that $G^*$ is not the communication graph (e.g., a vertex of $G$ is mapped to multiple vertices of $G^*$), and that the diameter of $G^*$ can be much larger than $D$ (i.e., possibly by a linear factor).
elements.sent-2411-013: We overcome these challenges by carefully defining and maintaining subgraphs of the dual graph $G^*$ while applying the recursive decomposition on the primal graph $G$.
elements.sent-2411-014: The main technical difficulty, is that along the recursive decomposition, a face of $G$ gets shattered into (disconnected) components yet we still need to treat it as a dual node.
elements.sent-2411-015: Distributed algorithms for network optimization problems have a long and rich history.
elements.sent-2411-016: These problems are commonly studied under the $\Congest$ model \cite{peleg-book where the network is abstracted as an $n$-vertex graph $G = (V, E)$ with hop-diameter $D$; communications occur in synchronous rounds, and per round, $O(\log n)$ bits can be sent along each edge.}
elements.sent-2411-017: A sequence of breakthrough results provided $\tilde{O (D+\sqrt{n )$-round algorithms for fundamental graph problems, such as minimum spanning tree (MST) \cite{GarayKP98 , approximate shortest-paths \cite{Nanongkai14 , minimum cuts \cite{DEMN21 , and approximate flow \cite{ghaffari2015near .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: For general graphs, $\tilde{O (D+\sqrt{n )$ rounds for solving the above mentioned problems is known to be near optimal, existentially \cite{SarmaHKKNPPW11 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: A major and concentrated effort has been invested in designing improved solutions for special graph families that escape the topology of the worst-case lower bound graphs of \cite{SarmaHKKNPPW11 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: The lower bound graph is sparse, and of arboricity two, so it belongs to many graph families.
elements.sent-2411-021: Arguably, one of the most interesting non-trivial families that escapes it, is the family of planar graphs.
elements.sent-2411-022: Thus, a significant focus has been given to the family of planar graphs, due to their frequent appearance in practice and because of their rich structural properties.
elements.sent-2411-023: In their seminal work, Ghaffari and Haeupler \cite{GH16a,GH16b initiated the line of distributed planar graph algorithms based on the notion of {\em low-congestion shortcuts .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: The latter serves the communication backbone for obtaining $\tilde{O (D)$-round algorithms for MST \cite{GH16b , minimum cut \cite{GH16b,GZ22 and approximate shortest paths \cite{GHSYZ22,GHLRZ22 in planar graphs.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: An additional key tool in working with planar graphs, starting with the seminal work of Lipton and Tarjan \cite{LT79 , is that of a planar separator path: a path whose removal from the graph leaves connected components that are a constant factor smaller.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Ghaffari and Parter \cite{GP17 presented a $\tilde{O (D)$-round randomized algorithm for computing a cycle separator of size $O(D)$ which consists of a separator path plus one additional edge (that is possibly a {\em virtual edge that is not in $G$).}}}

elements.sent-2411-029: Our goal in this paper is to expand the algorithmic toolkit for performing computation on the dual graph.
elements.sent-2411-030: This allows us to exploit the profound algorithmic duality in planar graphs, in which solving a problem $A$ in the dual graph provides a solution for problem $B$ in the primal graph.
elements.sent-2411-031: Within this context, our focus is on the Maximum $st$-Flow problem (in directed planar graphs with edge capacities).
elements.sent-2411-032: The Maximum $st$-Flow problem is arguably one of the most classical problems in theoretical computer science, extensively studied since the 50's, and still admitting breakthrough results in the sequential setting, such as the recent almost linear time algorithm by Chen, Kyng, Liu, Peng, Gutenberg and Sachdeva~\cite{ChenKLPGS22 .}

elements.sent-2411-231: We denote by $G=(V,E)$ the directed (possibly weighted) simple planar network of communication, and by $D$ the network's undirected and unweighted (hop) diameter.
elements.sent-2411-232: Let $S$ be a set of vertices or edges, we denote by $G[S]$ the subgraph of $G$ induced by $S$.
elements.sent-2411-233: We work in the standard distributed $\Congest$ model~\cite{peleg-book .}
elements.sent-2411-234: Initially, each vertex knows only its unique $O(\log n)$-bit identifier and the identifiers of its neighbors.
elements.sent-2411-236: In each round, each vertex can send (and receive) an $O(\log n)$-bit message on each of its incident edges (different edges can transmit different messages).
elements.sent-2411-237: When the edges of $G$ are weighted we assume that the weights are polynomially bounded integers.
elements.sent-2411-238: Thus, the weight of an edge can be transmitted in $O(1)$ rounds.
elements.sent-2411-239: This is a standard assumption in the $\Congest$ model.. {\bf Distributed storage.}
elements.sent-2411-240: When referring to a distributed algorithm that solves some problem on a network $G$, the input (and later the output) is stored distributively.
elements.sent-2411-241: That is, each vertex knows only a small (local) part of the information.
elements.sent-2411-242: e.g. when computing a shortest paths tree from a single source vertex $s$, we assume that all vertices know (as an input) the ID of $s$ and the weights and direction of their incident edges.
elements.sent-2411-243: When the algorithm halts, each vertex knows its distance from $s$ and which of its incident edges are in the shortest paths tree.. {\bf Planar embedding.}
elements.sent-2411-244: Let $G = (V, E)$ be a directed planar graph.
elements.sent-2411-245: The {\em geometric planar embedding of $G$ is a drawing of $G$ on a plane so that edges intersect only in vertices.}
elements.sent-2411-246: A {\em combinatorial planar embedding of $G$ provides for each $v\in G$, the local clockwise order of its incident edges, such that, the ordering of edges is consistent with some geometric planar embedding of $G$.}
elements.sent-2411-247: Throughout, we assume that a combinatorial embedding of $G$ is known locally for each vertex.
elements.sent-2411-248: This is done in $\tilde{O (D)$ rounds using the planar embedding algorithm of Ghaffari and Haeupler~\cite{GH16a .}}
elements.sent-2411-249: The {\em dual of the {\em primal planar graph $G$ is a planar graph, denoted $G^*$.}}
elements.sent-2411-250: The nodes of $G^*$ correspond to the faces of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-251: The dual graph has an edge $e^*$ for each pair of faces in $G$ that share an edge $e\in G$ (and a self-loop when the same face appears on both sides of an edge).
elements.sent-2411-252: If $e$ is directed then the direction of $e^*$ is from the face on the left of $e$ to the face on the right of $e$ where left and right are defined with respect to the direction of $e$.
elements.sent-2411-253: Observe that if two faces of $G$ share several edges then $G^*$ has several parallel edges between these faces.
elements.sent-2411-254: i.e., $G^*$ might be a multi-graph even when the primal graph $G$ is a simple graph.. See \cref{fig:dual_graph .}
elements.sent-2411-255: Since the mapping between edges of $G$ and $G^*$ is a bijection, we sometimes abuse notation and refer to both $e$ and $e^*$ as $e$.
elements.sent-2411-256: We refer to the vertices of $G$ as {\em vertices and to the vertices of $G^*$ as {\em nodes .}}

elements.sent-2411-257: We use the well-known duality between primal cycles and dual cuts: \begin{fact [Cycle-Cut Duality] A set of edges $C$ is a simple cycle in a connected planar graph $G$ if and only if $C$ is a simple cut in $G^*$ (a cut $C$ is said to be simple if when removed, the resulting graph has exactly two connected components).}

elements.sent-2411-260: To do so efficiently we want faces to be vertex-disjoint.
elements.sent-2411-261: To overcome these problems, the {\em face disjoint graph $\hat{G}$ was presented in \cite{GP17 as a way for simulating aggregations on the dual graph $G^*$ in the distributed setting, which we slightly modify here.}}
elements.sent-2411-262: As it is mainly a communication tool, we think of $\hat{G}$ as an undirected unweighted graph, however, it will still allow working with weighted and directed input graphs $G$, as each endpoint of an edge in $\hat{G}$ that represents an edge of $G^*$, shall know the weight and direction of that edge.
elements.sent-2411-263: Intuitively, $\hat{G}$ can be thought of as the result of duplicating all edges of $G$ so that faces of $G$ map to distinct faces of $\hat{G}$ that are both vertex and edge disjoint.
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=0.7\linewidth]{face_disjoint_graph_preliminaries.pdf} \caption{On the left, the primal graph $G$. On the right, the face-disjoint graph $\hat{G}$. Black edges in $\hat{G}$ are duplicates of $G$ edges, such that, connected components induced by them map to faces of $G$, e.g., the face $f_\infty$ of $G$ maps to the component $C_\infty$ in $\hat{G}$ (thick black). Dashed blue edges connect each star-center to other copies of the same vertex and solid blue edges connect two components if and only if there corresponding faces should be connected in $G^*$. For example, there are two such edges connecting $C_\infty$ to the component that maps to the face which contains $w$. } \label{fig: face_disjoint_graph_preliminaries}

elements.sent-2411-321: The root of $\mathcal{T}$ corresponds to the entire graph $G$, and the leaves of $\mathcal{T}$ correspond to subgraphs of small size $\tilde{O (D)$.}
elements.sent-2411-322: In \cite{LP19 , each bag $X$ was defined as a subset of vertices.}
elements.sent-2411-323: For our purposes, it is convenient to define $X$ as a subset of edges (a subgraph).
elements.sent-2411-324: Given that we want to work with the dual graph $G^*$, the bijection between the edges of $G^*$ and the edges of $G$ allows us to move smoothly between working on $G$ and $G^*$.
elements.sent-2411-325: In this section, our goal is to extend the BDD of \cite{LP19 to the dual network $G^*$.}
elements.sent-2411-326: We stress that the extension to the BDD is not obtained by a simple black-box application of the BDD on $G^*$ (for the two reasons mentioned earlier: $G^*$ is not the network of communication, and $G^*$'s diameter might be much larger than the diameter of $G$).
elements.sent-2411-327: Instead, we use the same BDD $\mathcal T$ and carefully define the duals $X^*$ of bags $X$.
elements.sent-2411-328: We begin with the following lemma summarizing the properties of the BDD of \cite{LP19 .}
elements.sent-2411-329: There is a distributed randomized $\tilde{O (D)$-rounds algorithm that w.h.p.}

elements.sent-2411-331: The leaves of \ $\mathcal{T}$ correspond to graphs of size $O(D \log n)$.
elements.sent-2411-332: For a non-leaf bag $X$, let $\SX$ be the set of vertices of $X$ which are present in more than one child bag of $X$.
elements.sent-2411-334: For every bag $X$, the diameter of $X$ is at most $O(D \log n)$.
elements.sent-2411-335: For every bag $X$ with child bags $X_1,X_2,\ldots$, we have $X=\cup_i X_i$.
elements.sent-2411-336: Each edge $e$ of $G$ is in at most two distinct bags of the same level of $\mathcal{T}$.
elements.sent-2411-337: Every bag has a unique $\tilde{O (1)$-bit ID.}
elements.sent-2411-338: Every vertex knows the IDs of all bags containing it.

elements.sent-2411-340: Since our focus is on the dual graph, we need to understand how faces of $G$ are affected by the BDD.
elements.sent-2411-341: Namely, decomposing a bag $X$ into child bags $X_1,X_2,\ldots$ might partition some faces of $X$ into multiple parts, where each such {\em face-part is contained in a distinct child bag of $X$.}

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Dual Distance Labeling (high-level description) } \label{algorithm_dual_labeling} \KwIn{A BDD of a $D$-diameter network $G$ satisfying the properties of \cref{theorem_additional_BDD_properties}.} \KwResult{Each vertex $v$ of $G$ learns $\Label(g)$ in $G^*$ for each face $g$ of $G$ that contains it. } \ForAll{bags $X$ of the same level, bottom-up, in parallel, }{ \If{$X$ is a leaf-bag}{ Collect the entire graph $X$ in each vertex $v\in X$\; } \Else(\tcc*[f]{Non-leaf bags}){ \tcc*[f]{Broadcast step} Broadcast the dual edges of $\SX$ edges that exist in $X^*$ to the entire bag $X$\; Broadcast, in two steps, the labels computed in the previous level (in child bags of $X^*$) of faces (face-parts) that correspond to $\FX$ to the entire bag $X$. \tcc*[f]{Local Step} Each vertex $v\in X$ uses the received information in the above broadcast in order to locally construct the Dense Distance Graph for each face (face-part) $g$ that contains it in $X$\; } Using the previously received information, each vertex $v\in X$ computes locally $\Label_{X^*}(g)$ for each face (face-part) $g$ of $X$ that contains it or reports a negative cycle aborting the algorithm\; }

elements.sent-2411-751: Initially, nodes of the input graph shall know the source $s$, the approximation parameter $\epsilon$ and a trivial estimate $\Delta$ of the maximum distance in the graph ($\Delta:=2nW$ in our case), then, each node computes additional parameters that depend only on the former.
elements.sent-2411-752: Note, all of the former may be implemented in only one minor-aggregation round.
elements.sent-2411-753: Next, the algorithm computes a distance approximation, based on a call to the approximation oracle (any SSSP approximation algorithm, without any additional special guarantees) with $s$ and $\epsilon'=O(\epsilon/ \log n)$ .
elements.sent-2411-754: Then, sequentially, $O(\log n)$ phases are performed, each defining a distance approximation, such that, the final output of the algorithm is the approximation computed in the last phase.
elements.sent-2411-755: In each phase, two calls to the approximate oracle are performed with $s$ and $\epsilon'/100$.
elements.sent-2411-756: Each call to the oracle is on a {\em level graph .}
elements.sent-2411-757: Afterwards, each level graph defines a distance approximation of the input graph, and the phase's distance approximation is computed by taking the element- (node-) wise minimum over: (1) the approximations computed on the level graphs, and (2) the approximation from the previous phase.
elements.sent-2411-758: Again, taking the minimum requires only one minor-aggregation round.. \em Level graphs.
elements.sent-2411-759: The only vagueness in the implementation lies in the level graphs, which we now define, then, show that they can constructed and simulated efficiently in the minor-aggregation model.
elements.sent-2411-760: The level graph $H$ (Definition 5.7 in \cite{RozhonHMGZ23 ) in a specific phase of an input graph $\mathcal{G}$ where each node of $\mathcal{G}$ knows its (approximate) distance from a source $s$, is defined as follows.}
elements.sent-2411-761: The node and edge sets of $H$ are the same as those of $\mathcal{G}$, then: (1) Each node assigns itself a level (a number), depending only on its distance from $s$ and some parameter $\omega$, (2) each edge that connects neighbors of different levels is removed, the weights of edges kept are altered depending only on previously computed parameters that all nodes of $\mathcal{G}$ know.
elements.sent-2411-762: Finally, (3) An edge between $s$ and each $u\in \mathcal{G}$ is added, with weight depending only on $\omega$ and the $s$-to-$u$ distance approximated in the previous phase.
elements.sent-2411-763: First, we start by computing $\omega$ in one minor-aggregation round on $G^*_{virt}$ as it depends only on parameters known to all nodes of the input graph (as $\Delta, \epsilon$ and others).
elements.sent-2411-764: Then, we compute the level of each node, also in one round, as it only depends on $\omega$ and the $s$-to-$u$ distance approximated in the previous phase.
elements.sent-2411-765: The remaining things to explain are: (1) How to "delete" edges that cross levels, and (2) How to connect $s$ to all nodes with the appropriate edge weights.
elements.sent-2411-766: Deactivating edges that cross levels is simple.
elements.sent-2411-767: Each edge $e=(u,v)$ in the input graph knows the level of both its endpoints, thus, it knows if it should participate in the level graph or not.
elements.sent-2411-768: In more detail, recall, $e$ in the minor aggregation model is simulated by its endpoints $u',v'$ in the base communication graph ($\hat{G}$ in our case).
elements.sent-2411-769: In one round on $\hat{G}$, $u'$ knows the level of the super-node that contains $v'$ (and vise-versa).
elements.sent-2411-770: Thus, $u',v'$ know that $e$ is prevented from participating in computations on $H$.
elements.sent-2411-771: In case $v'$ is virtual then all nodes of the graph simulate it (in particular $u'$), and know its information (in particular, its level).
elements.sent-2411-772: Luckily, we work in the extended minor-aggregation model, where adding virtual nodes and connecting them arbitrarily to any other virtual or non-virtual node, is a feature of the model.
elements.sent-2411-773: In the same manner that we described earlier (where we obtained $G^*_{virt}$), we alter $H$ and replace $s$ with a virtual node, all nodes in $H$ now know they are connected to the source.
elements.sent-2411-774: Notice, there are no problems regarding weights of edges, as the weight of an added edge $(s,u)$ depends only on $\omega$ and the approximated $s$-to-$u$ distance in the previous phase (already known to $u$).
elements.sent-2411-775: Thus, when nodes connect themselves to the replaced $s$, they connect themselves with the needed weights.
elements.sent-2411-776: Again, virtual nodes are simulated by all vertices, so for each virtual $v$, all real nodes know its info and locally consider it to be connected to $s$ with the right weight.
elements.sent-2411-777: Note, each phase is of $\tilde{O (1)+T_{SSSP(\epsilon')}$ minor-aggregation rounds, where $T_{SSSP(\epsilon')}$ denotes the minor-aggregation round complexity of a $(1+\epsilon')$-approximate SSSP oracle.}
elements.sent-2411-778: Thus, the overall minor-aggregate round complexity of the $(1+\epsilon)$ smooth approximate distance computation is $O(\log n)\cdot \tilde{O (T_{SSSP(\epsilon') )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-779: Implementing the approximate SSSP oracle with the algorithm of \cite{GHSYZ22 , results with a total minor-aggregate round complexity of $O(\log n)\cdot (\frac{1 {\epsilon' )^2\cdot 2^{O(\log n \cdot \log \log n)^{3/4 }$, i.e., $\frac{\log^3 n {\epsilon^2 \cdot 2^{O(\log n \cdot \log \log n)^{3/4 }$, which translates to $\frac{D {\epsilon^2}\cdot n^{o(1)}$ $\Congest$ rounds on the network of communication $G$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-780: For conclusion, since each vertex $v$ in $G$ simulates its copies in $\hat{G}$, which in turn simulate the faces $v$ participates in in $G^*_{virt}$, then, $v$ knows for each face it participates in, its distance from the source.

elements.sent-2411-782: Finally, we are ready to go through the last modification required to keep our promise, that is, we show next how to allow zero capacities (zero-weight edges in the dual shortest paths approximation).
elements.sent-2411-783: The algorithm of~\cite{GHSYZ22 that we use for the approximate SSSP and of~\cite{RozhonHMGZ23 used to obtain the smoothness property work for weights in $[1,n^{O(1) ]$, indeed all weights in $G^*_{virt}$, even that of the infinite capacity edge are in $[1,n^{O(1) ]$ except the zero-weighted ones.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-784: To deal with that issue, we simulate the obtained smooth approximate SSSP algorithm above while considering zero weighted edges as follows: We run the algorithm on maximal connected components of $G^*_{virt}\setminus\{e:w(e)=0\}$, obtaining a partial SSSP tree of $G^*_{virt}$, then we augment it with some zero weighted edges without closing a cycle, obtaining an SSSP tree $T$ of $G^*_{virt}$, all done via the extended minor aggregation model.
elements.sent-2411-785: Concretely, we work in two phases.
elements.sent-2411-786: In the first phase, we compute an approximate SSSP tree $T_1$ on the minor $G^*_1$ of $G^*_{virt}$ where all zero-weighted edges are contracted, where the source is the super-node that contains $f_1$.
elements.sent-2411-787: Now, we need to choose what zero-weighted edges shall be added to complete the approximate SSSP tree $T_1$ of $G^*_1$ into an approximate SSSP tree $T$ of $G^*_{virt}$.
elements.sent-2411-788: i.e., we are looking for some spanning tree in $G_2$.
elements.sent-2411-789: So, we compute a minimum spanning tree $T_2$ of $G^*_2$ by a minor aggregation procedure of \cite{GHSYZ22 (Example 4.4 in their paper).}
elements.sent-2411-790: Hence, the tree $T=T_1\cup T_2$ (considering the endpoints of edges of $T_1,T_2$ in $G^*_{virt}$ rather than $G^*_1,G^*_2$ when taking the union) constitutes an approximate SSSP tree in $G^*_{virt}$.
elements.sent-2411-791: Note, $T$ obeys the triangle-inequality (which is crucial for a flow assignment) for the following reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-792: Distances in $T_1$ represent distances in $G^*_{virt}$, by construction any pair of nodes in $T_1$ satisfies the triangle inequality.
elements.sent-2411-793: Thus, map each node $f\in G^*_{virt}$ to the super-node $a$ of $T_1$ that contains it, the property follows since $f$ and $a$ have the same distances from the source because $T_1$ was obtained by contracting zero-weight edges only.
elements.sent-2411-794: I.e, $T=T_1\cup T_2$ satisfies the triangle inequality as any pair of (super-)nodes in $T_1$ does.. \qedhere \end{proof.}

elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Distributed Knowledge of Faces} \label{algorithm_distributed_knowledge_of_faces} \KwIn{A BDD $\mathcal{T}$ of a $D$-diameter network $G$ and a BFS tree $T_{\!_X}$ for each $X\in\mathcal{T}$.} \KwResult{Each vertex $v$ of $G$ learns for each incident edge copy the list of faces and face-parts that contains it in each bag $X\in\mathcal{T}$ that contains it.} Each vertex learns for each of its incident edge copies the list of bags that contains it (\cref{lemma_know_darts})\; Each vertex learns for each of its incident edge copies the list of faces that contain it in bag $X=G$ (\cref{lemma_know_darts_G})\; \ForAll{bags $X$ of level $\ell\in [2,\Depth(\mathcal{T})]$, in parallel, }{ \tcc*[f]{Global step} Detect the critical face (face-part) of $X$ if exists and broadcast a message on $T$ notifying all vertices that it shall be partitioned\; For each edge that participates in a face-part of $X$, broadcast the ID of the face-part and the child bag that contains it to the root of $T$\; The root of $T$ broadcasts to all vertices of $X$ the IDs of face(-parts) that shall be partitioned in between child bags of $X^*$\; \tcc*[f]{Local Step} Each vertex of $X$ learns locally for each incident edge copy the new ID of each face(-part) that contains it in each child bag of $X^*$ that contains it (if any)\; }

elements.section-2411-010: Deferred Details and Proofs from Section~\ref{section_flow_cut}
elements.sent-2411-1168: The algorithm follows the centralized algorithm of Miller and Naor~\cite{MN95 .}
elements.sent-2411-1169: It works by performing a binary search on the value $\lambda$ of the maximum $st$-flow.
elements.sent-2411-1170: In each iteration, $\lambda$ units of flow are pushed along a (not necessarily shortest) $s$-to-$t$ path of darts $P$.
elements.sent-2411-1171: Otherwise, a residual graph is defined by subtracting $\lambda$ from the capacity of every dart in $P$ and adding $\lambda$ to their reverse dart (in the beginning every edge $e$ of capacity $c(e)$ corresponds to two darts - one in the direction of $e$ with capacity $c(e)$, and its reverse with capacity zero).
elements.sent-2411-1172: Notice that residual capacities may be negative for the darts of $P$.
elements.sent-2411-1173: To fix this, an arbitrary face $f$ of $G$ is chosen and SSSP (with positive and negative edge lengths) in $G^*$ is computed from $f$.
elements.sent-2411-1174: Miller and Naor~\cite{MN95 showed that if a negative cycle is detected then we can conclude that the maximum $st$-flow in $G$ is smaller than $\lambda$, and otherwise it is at least $\lambda$.}

Vertex $v$ in $G$ has a star-center copy $v$ in $V_s\subset V(\hat{G})$, and $\deg(v)$ additional duplicates $v_0,v_1,v_2$ in $V(\hat{G})$ connected by star edges $E_S \subset E(\hat{G})$ (dashed blue). elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{Distributed Knowledge of Faces} \label{algorithm_distributed_knowledge_of_faces} \KwIn{A BDD $\mathcal{T}$ of a $D$-diameter network $G$ and a BFS tree $T_{\!_X}$ for each $X\in\mathcal{T}$.} \KwResult{Each vertex $v$ of $G$ learns for each incident dart $d$ the list of faces and face-parts that contain $d$ in each bag $X\in\mathcal{T}$ that contains $d$.} Each vertex learns for each incident dart $d$ the list of bags that contains $d$ (\cref{lemma_know_darts})\; Each vertex learns for each incident dart $d$ the list of faces that contain $d$ in the bag $X=G$ (\cref{lemma_know_darts_G})\; \ForAll{bags $X$ of level $\ell\in [2,\Depth(\mathcal{T})]$, in parallel, }{ \tcc*[f]{Global step} Detect the critical face (face-part) of $X$ if exists and broadcast a message on $T$ notifying all vertices that it shall be partitioned\; For each edge that participates in a face-part of $X$, broadcast the ID of the face-part and the child bag that contains it to the root of $T$\; The root of $T$ broadcasts to all vertices of $X$ the IDs of face(-parts) that shall be partitioned in between child bags of $X^*$\; \tcc*[f]{Local Step} Each vertex of $X$ learns locally for each incident dart $d$ the new ID of each face(-part) that contains $d$ in each child bag of $X^*$ that contains $d$ (if any)\; }

elements.algorithm-2411-004: \caption{Dual Distance Labeling (high-level description) } \label{algorithm_dual_labeling} \KwIn{A BDD of a $D$-diameter network $G$ satisfying the properties of \cref{theorem_additional_BDD_properties}.} \KwResult{Each vertex $v$ of $G$ learns $\Label(g)$ in $G^*$ for each face $g$ of $G$ that contains it. } \ForAll{bags $X$ of level $\ell\in [\Depth(\mathcal{T}),1]$, in parallel, }{ \If{$X$ is a leaf-bag}{ Collect the entire graph $X$ in each vertex $v\in X$\; } \Else(\tcc*[f]{Non-leaf bags}){ \tcc*[f]{Broadcast step} Broadcast the dual edges of $\SX$ edges that exist in $X^*$ to the entire bag $X$\; Broadcast, in two steps, the labels computed in the previous level (in child bags of $X^*$) of faces (face-parts) that correspond to $\FX$ to the entire bag $X$. \tcc*[f]{Local Step} Each vertex $v\in X$ uses the received information in the above broadcast in order to locally construct the Dense Distance Graph for each face (face-part) $g$ that contains it in $X$\; } Each vertex $v\in X$ computes locally $\Label_{X^*}(g)$ for each face (face-part) $g$ of $X$ that contains it or reports a negative cycle aborting the algorithm\; }
